title: AMA: Randle cycle, Fruit and NAFLD, Contaminated Avocado/Olive oil, PFAs, Deer liver and Pregnancy

chunks.title: Introduction to Whole Package Supplement
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the Whole Package supplement from Heart and Soil, highlighting its key ingredients and personal benefits such as increased energy and improved recovery. They emphasize the nutritional value of animal-based diets, particularly the inclusion of organ meats like testicles, liver, and blood.

chunks.title: Ask Me Anything Podcast Introduction
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces an 'Ask Me Anything' format for the podcast, outlining various topics that will be addressed including dietary questions related to pregnancy, kids, and specific food items like avocado oil. They invite further questions from listeners for future episodes.
chunks.transcript: this week on the podcast i wanted to do an ask me anything so many of you guys have asked so many good questions in the past this week i will address things like what about the randall cycle paul what about the hards of teeth am i just addressing those people who are metabolically healthy how should you eat for pregnancy what about breastfeeding what about kids what about deer liver is it okay to eat deer liver what about avocado oil i show a new study with this one what about the recent study that came out showing that food worsens non-alcoholic fatty liver disease i address all of these in today's ama podcast it seems like you guys like these they're super fun to do because i get to talk about all different stuff and throw out lots of articles so enjoy this ask me anything podcast if you guys have more questions for future amas feel free to put them below i definitely read the youtube comments and have tried to answer some of the most common youtube questions and confusions and concerns or even questions from my dms on instagram you guys can all follow me there i also want to point out animal based 30 our free 30-day animal-based challenge with heart and soil supplements starts august the 1st you can go to animalbase30.com to sign up if you're seeing this after august the 1st which most of you will probably are then you can still go to animal base 30. you can sign up free all month join us there's a free ebook all kinds of resources a facebook group we just want more people to benefit from eating an animal based diet of organs meat fruit honey and raw dairy join us join me join heart and soil supplements animalbase30.com on to today's podcast

chunks.title: Discussion on Fruit and Liver Health Study
chunks.summary: The speaker reviews a study examining the effects of fruit consumption on liver health, specifically non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD). They critique the study's methodology regarding fruit definitions and raise concerns about its implications for dietary advice, especially in the context of ketogenic diets.
chunks.transcript: podcast with an article that a lot of people have been asking me about it's an article from i believe this group is in iran and i will show it on the screen title of the study is the effect of a fruit rich diet on liver biomarkers insulin resistance and lipid profile in patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease randomized controlled trials so you can imagine that if a lot of people have been sending this to me the results raise an eyebrow so if you read the results here what they say is the results of the present study indicate the consumption of fruits more than four servings per day exacerbates steatosis which is fat in the liver dyslipidemia glycemic control in nafld patients patients with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease fewer further studies are needed to identify the underlying mechanisms of the effect of fruits on nafld so this got me thinking this doesn't make a whole lot of sense to me i've seen other studies that show that fruit doesn't worsen insulin resistance and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease is a disease of insulin resistance at its core that is really uh that is essentially a circular definition of nafld nafld is a manifestation of insulin resistance so why would there be some studies that show that fruit doesn't worsen insulin resistance those i've shown in the past rick johnson studies where you can give people fruit as they are losing weight and it doesn't abrogate you don't lose the benefits of a processed fructose restricted diet so why does this study show that fruit worsens nafld well i got my answer a little later on when i read the study a little more carefully if let's just start with this piece of the study so what they said is the participants randomly assigned to the group uh consumption of at least four servings of fruits daily or a control group with fruit consumption at less than two servings a day so we have four and less than two okay and they looked at all these markers of steatosis liver inflammation nafld severity insulin resistance so if you read the paper and you look at the methods what you will find is that their definition of fruit is a little bit shady so you can see here the the category of fruits was based on colored fruits dried fruits that's a little sketchy and other fruits well what exactly are other fruits the investigators don't say but i have some real qualms with this the first qualm is that we know that many dried fruits are going to contain sugar you have only to go to your grocery store to find dried cranberries dried blueberries many dried fruits contain sugar processed sugar which we know worsens nafld non-alcoholic fatty liver disease not surprisingly and then what are other fruits what exactly are other fruits is this peaches in a can that have added sugar is this a fruit roll-up which has added sugar we don't even know what other fruits are and to me this makes it pretty darn suspect when we're looking at the results of this study and it makes me wonder if we can really say that a banana a mango a papaya a pineapple are going to worsen nafld and humans we can't say that until we have further studies and until we really know what these other fruits are and we should have excluded dried fruits that have added processed sugar this to me is really frustrating this is the problem with most nutritional research that unless you read between the lines you will not fully understand the study

chunks.title: Criticism of Olive Oil Consumption
chunks.summary: The speaker responds to questions about olive oil, sharing concerns over its health implications due to adulteration and high peroxide values. They recommend animal fats instead, arguing that tallow and butter are superior for human health.
chunks.transcript: the details and disappointingly there are a few people in the keto community who are using this study as evidence and holding it up to say see fruit isn't good for nafld fruit isn't chunks.title: Intermittent Fasting Insights
chunks.summary: The speaker provides opinions on intermittent fasting, discussing its potential benefits and drawbacks, especially concerning hormonal health. They suggest that caloric restriction may not be the optimal approach for weight loss.

chunks.title: Nutrition for Pregnancy and Infants
chunks.summary: Addressing questions about dietary recommendations for pregnant women and infants, the speaker advocates for an animal-based diet rich in organ meats, emphasizing the importance of specific nutrients like choline and iron during pregnancy and early childhood.

essentially a question from caitlyn she says paul i've been hearing a lot about the randall cycle i don't fully understand the biochemistry does the randall cycle mean that we shouldn't eat carbohydrates and fat together is this going to make me fat if i do this how should i navigate eating fruit on my animal-based diet so i think there's a lot of people in the dogmatic carnivore community chirping about randall cycle randall cycle when i am talking about including fruit on my animal-based diet but if you really look at the biochemistry underlying the randle cycle this doesn't make a whole lot of sense so let's just start with what the randall cycle is it's essentially substrate level competition that prefers to use the fuel that is already being used in human biochemistry so this means that when you are doing glucose metabolism which most of us are doing if we are eating any carbohydrates in a meal the breakdown products of the krebs cycle including malonyl coa from glucose metabolism inhibit carnitine palmitoel transferase this is part of the system that shuttles fatty acids toward beta oxidation in the mitochondria similarly and conversely high concentrations of acetyl coa and citrate from beta oxidation which is fatty acid oxidation inhibit the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex that shuttles glucose metabolites toward the krebs cycle in our biochemistry this is the preference for using the fuel that is already in use so what are we talking about here we're basically talking about the fact that if you are metabolizing glucose then you're not going to be in ketosis and doing beta oxidation these are not mutually exclusive and you can do beta oxidation you can use fatty acids when you are not necessarily in ketosis but if you listen to the podcast i did with thomas lauer you know that when you are in ketosis when you are using beta oxidation when you are shuttling fatty acids non-esterified fatty acids which are in your blood through the mitochondria through carnitine pulmonary oil transferase through beta oxidation you are in ketosis a lot of the time and that process is going to create physiologic insulin resistance at the level of the muscle so you're refusing glucose so basically if you're in ketosis if you don't have carbohydrates your body is going to spare glucose for the tissues that really need it the brain the testicles the adrenals and if you have glucose in the environment as i've said on previous podcasts this is really a signal of abundance for humans and the body's not going to burn fatty acids in the sense of it's not going to make non-sterified fatty acids from the fat tissue it's not going to increase not a certified fatty acids in the blood it's not going to create physiologic peripheral insulin resistance in that state of ketosis because it can use glucose now your body goes back and forth with these throughout the day to differing degrees we must be doing some level of beta oxidation throughout the day it's not an on or off switch but these processes are in competition to some degree which is normal human biochemistry i think most of the time you don't want to be in ketosis you want your muscles to be insulin sensitive you want the glucose disposal you want the glucose disposal to be happening at the level of your muscles as you know if you've listened to my podcast on ketosis why i'm not a fan the recent one with thomas de lauer when you are in ketosis your fasting blood sugar will be higher because you are physiologically insulin resistant you're not a certified fatty acids in the blood will be higher if you listen to my blood work podcast you know that my non-certified fatty acids are very low right now 0.12 because i am including carbohydrates in my diet when i was on a ketogenic diet my nanocere fatty acids were much higher 0.6 this is physiologic insulin resistance this is essentially what the randall cycle is talking about but i think this gets conflated and misunderstood a lot in these dare i say dogmatic ketogenic dogmatic carnivore communities to say that you will get fat if you eat fruit and animal fat together and i am living proof of that as i am ballooning and getting super fat this is obviously a joke so what we can see very clearly from thousands of people who are eating an animal-based diet we hope that thousands more will join us for animal-based 30 at heart and soil that when you eat an animal-based diet a lot of people lose weight most people are going to lose weight they're going to become more jacked they're going to become more lean so clearly eating fruit and animal fat together doesn't make you fat that's not what the randall cycle is really saying it's not saying that if i eat a steak with some fat along with fruit that that fat is going to get stuck on my body and i'm going to become fat yes my body may preference burning the glucose during that meal but later on overnight i'm easily going to go into a fat burning mode i'm easily going to moderate my fat stores because i am an insulin sensitive metabolically healthy individual i think that many people who are thinking about this and worried about the randall cycle in terms of actual eating the actual eating of carbohydrates and fat together haven't read probably the original paper by pj randall which i'll show here on the screen the title is the glucose fatty acid cycle its role in insulin sensitivity and the metabolic disturbances of diabetes mellitus and if you read this he says we now wish to summarize evidence which appears to show that the high plasma concentrations of nifa which is the which are non-esterified fatty acids in parentheses or the underlying breakdown of glycerides of which it is a symptom stands in a causal relationship to these abnormalities of carbohydrate metabolism these abnormalities of carbohydrate metabolism being the things i mentioned earlier that these products of beta oxidation specifically citrate and acetyl coa do serve to inhibit pyruvate dehydrogenase and they go on to say and we suggest that this is a distinct biochemical syndrome which could appropriately be called the fatty acid syndrome we wish to propose further that there are interactions between glucose and fatty acid metabolism in muscle and adipose tissue which take the form of a cycle the glucose fatty acid cycle and which are fundamental to the control of glucose and fatty acid concentrations in the blood and of insulin sensitivity you can see here that they detail this glucose fatty acid cycle including fatty acid acyl coa glucose going to algosterol 3 phosphate triglycerides glycerol and fatty acids but the take-home message in this paper and in the actual understanding of this human biochemistry is that the products of human fatty acid metabolism are going to favor the continuation of fatty acid metabolism well that sounds great we should all be in ketosis all the time we'll burn all of our fat except we won't and we'll get electrolyte problems testosterone issues all the things i've talked about in the past there's a balance here it is okay to eat carbohydrates from fruit with your fat this doesn't mean that you will get fat i'm clearly not a fat individual i'm very lean if you've seen any of my instagram or other social media where i frequently have my shirt off because i'm often surfing or just in costa rica because i'm in a warm place but this isn't the way it ends up being in human biochemistry this is a misunderstanding of the randall cycle the randall cycle is talking about the reasons we become physiologically insulin resistant when you avoid carbohydrates and the fact that when you add back carbohydrates that process will then damp down fatty acid metabolism that's normal human biochemistry we favor glucose in our metabolism that's fine you won't get fat so hopefully that's an explanation of you guys of the randall cycle why i don't fear it i think this has been widely misunderstood widely misinterpreted and all of the chirping in the community just needs to stop because they don't understand what they're talking about and that's there's thousands of cases of living proof of that in humans today the next question i want to go to is from nick he says i'm an avid outdoorsman and health conscious individual i've begun reading paul's book and have been listening to countless you really want to have optimal health you really must take the next steps to understand the quality of the food you are eating so they say scientists are still working to determine the source of pfas and then also if they're being fed to cattle and livestock as a part of poor quality feed that's going to increase the pfas's in the food chain now let's talk about chicken and pork you guys may know what are the chickens you're eating fed well if they're fed regular chicken feed we know that that chicken feed is probably containing seed oils is it does it contain pieces of plastic or scraps of paper is it low quality what is the what are the pfas what are the pfas levels in that chicken feed how much of that pfas contamination is ending up in the eggs it's all quite possible so i say this not to make you afraid i say this to empower you with knowledge i believe that in the coming future we will increasingly hear about pfases i think these are one of the greatest threats to our health seed oils processed sugars biggest first threats but pfases are the new bpas and even with bpas bisphenol a there are now bpe and bps and these other bisphenol compounds that are similarly endocrine disruptive so these endocrine disruptors these environmental contaminants pfases bisphenol type compounds these are the things we must go to great lengths to avoid as much as possible if we truly want to be healthy as humans so this is a great question from nick while we are talking about raw milk i wanted to share a study that i found really interesting there are multiple studies like this in the literature um but as i was doing some research for content for instagram i came across a number of literature reviews and studies which are observational and i'll talk about that in a moment looking at raw milk so you can see in this study raw cow's milk and its protective effect on allergies and asthma and they found that they present the current understanding of the protective effect of raw milk on allergies and asthma there's a significant body of epidemiologic evidence that consumption of raw milk in childhood and adulthood in farm but also non-farm populations can be one of the most protective factors against allergy and asthma i think this probably has to do with the probiotic compounds found in raw milk certainly when raw milk is not handled properly it can be a source of food contamination but for me if i can get it from a good source the benefits outweigh the risks if you can obtain raw milk that's a decision you have to make for yourself but there is really clear good evidence that raw milk is beneficial and protective for hay fever eczema asthma and other allergic conditions probably because being exposed to those bacteria in the milk as a child and adult is beneficial for our immune system for our gut microbiome the delicate interplay of these things throughout our lives so raw milk is great understand the protective sourcing some people when i talk about this epidemiology with regard to raw milk will point out that i am not a fan of epidemiology with regard to vegetables and say look at this guy he's a hypocrite so i wanted to clarify that position i wanted to clarify that position in this moment as well when i look at epidemiology regarding vegetables there are a couple of factors to take into play that i think make that epidemiology highly suspect and highly at risk of being confounded by what we call healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias specifically if you look at the epidemiology regarding vegetables in human health or meat and human health there are disparate results across u.s western and eastern populations this makes me highly suspect there's something else going on i don't believe meat is good for asians and bad for the u.s and i don't believe that vegetables are necessarily good for humans based on epidemiology studies that could be confounded by what we call a healthy user bias there is a real compelling narrative that must be disproved with interventional studies here when we look at epidemiology we must be aware of the possibility of healthy and unhealthy user bias we must look at the people eating vegetables and ask is there a consistent narrative over the last 70 years i would argue there is that says vegetables are healthy so the people who eat more vegetables are also likely to be people who are doing more healthy behaviors could that be having an effect could that be making the vegetables look like they are beneficial to those people when in fact it is their healthy behaviors yes and if you've listened to any of my content you know there are many analyses of this epidemiology that shows exactly that specifically in the uk shopper study if you look at both vegetarians and omnivores we find that when the healthy behaviors are accounted for they both live the same amount of time meaning that there was no protective effect of eating more vegetables or excluding meat from the diet when they looked at which groups were doing healthy behaviors the omnivores who had healthy behaviors lived a long amount of time and the vegetarians who had healthy behaviors lived longer than the average person in the uk as well so the protective effect appears to be from the protective effect appears to be from healthy behaviors not necessarily from the absence of eating meat or from the inclusion of more vegetables in the human diet there are also many other multiple there are also many other studies i've talked about that are interventional studies looking at the inclusion of larger or significant amounts of fruit and vegetables in the diet versus lower amounts of fruit and vegetables or lower amount or zero fruit and vegetables in the human diet and there are many interventional studies which are much more revealing than these epidemiology studies which suggest that those vegetables are not necessarily protective for humans and the results of those studies many times suggest that that inclusion of fruit and vegetables didn't necessarily have a protective effect for those humans i've shown those interventional studies many times in the past so all of this data together makes me highly suspicious of the epidemiology regarding vegetables in human health and i think it is highly likely it is confounded by healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias when i look at epidemiology like this regarding raw milk and its protective effect on allergies and asthma the first thing i think is well maybe it's being on the farm maybe more people on the farm are drinking raw milk and this is a type of healthy user bias but in the abstract they say they have control for this and that people who are not on farms also see a benefit associated with raw milk so i can't come up with a more compelling hypothesis for why raw milk is protective for humans than the fact that it is actually protected for humans because of the probiotics in the milk the difference here is that when we are looking at epidemiology we must be careful to account for the possibility of healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias and when i look at epidemiology if i can't draw a compelling story or i can't figure out where the healthy user bias or unhealthy user bias could be confounding the study it makes me much more suspicious that it makes me much more confident that the association may be true the association still needs to be tested with interventional studies we can never draw causative inference from this observational research but it gives me more faith in the association in that situation and i may talk about it so this is the difference between vegetable studies that are epidemiological and things like raw milk if you guys have been following my content recently you know that i've been talking about an epidemiology study that linked that associated fish consumption with increased rates of melanoma let's use this one as another example here can i come up with a compelling narrative by which people who eat more fish may have an increased risk of melanoma i really can't perhaps you can you can put it in the comments there's probably a healthy user bias there the people that eat more fish probably believe that fish is healthy for them and yet they're having an unhealthy complication specifically increased rates of melanoma so where might the healthy user bias or the unhealthy user bias be hiding in this epidemiology i can't think of it which makes me find this association quite compelling and then we can say and there are many plausible explanations for the reason that fish might increase the rates of melanoma microplastics pfases and things like heavy metals or pcbs polychlorinated biphenyls in the fish that could be worsening cancer rates specifically melanoma so this finding was really taken with a lot of disbelief and incredulity by the nutrition community was widely ignored because everyone knows that fish is so healthy because red meat is so bad for you and i'm just laughing in the background thinking you guys actually need to realize that this fish is not good for humans and this is a good indication of this but i wish that most of this epidemiology were then followed up with interventional studies that's what we really need we need more interventional studies to test whether the inclusion of vegetables in the human diet is actually beneficial for humans or not we need more interventional studies to test whether meat is beneficial or harmful for humans i've shown in this podcast many times that they're that of the existing intervention studies we have red meat looks to be very healthy for humans no surprise there it's what we've been favoring for our entire existence as humans and we need more interventional studies to look at raw milk and see if it does protect humans for allergies and asthma the problem is that some of these studies must be done longitudinally which takes a long time we need to figure out how to address that in human health and nutritional research because so many of these associations can be valuable but we must be careful not to be fooled by healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias all right i'll do one more question to wrap up this ama for you guys this week steve asks how would you construct a diet and harden soil stack for a pregnant woman i think i had heard you mentioned bone marrow and liver is one of the recommendations would skin hair and nails or any others be advisable for prenatal as well as during pregnancy so i've done a podcast recently with lindsay milis in which we talk about a diocese in which we talk about dietary recommendations for prenatal conception pregnancy and postpartum i believe that an animal-based diet focused on organs and meat and fruit and honey is ideal across the lifespan i believe that an animal-based diet of organs and meat and fruit and honey is ideal across the human lifespan and that whether you're feeding a child or you're feeding a woman who's looking to conceive or a woman who is pregnant these are an ideal set of foods for those people now there's going to be some variation in terms of calories needed someone who is pregnant is going to be very careful not to eat too few calories and so they might want to increase the carbohydrates they might want to increase the fats but i think that as i've said in the past for a pregnant woman you want to think about one gram of protein per pound of body weight that is a combination of organs and meat and that is for goal body weight pregnant women should not be looking to lose weight so that when they get pregnant they're probably at their goal body weight one gram of protein per pound of body weight remember that one pound of meat is 100 grams of protein start there get organs a little bit of liver every day half an ounce a day totally great during pregnancy contains so many critical nutrients that are essential for the pregnancy folate riboflavin all of these things choline for the developing brain and the fetus a half an ounce of liver is not going to put anybody at risk of vitamin a toxicity get that liver from a good source as i mentioned in this podcast don't just get regular calves liver from walmart get a grass-fed grass finish regenerative farm liver even ask them for a coa a certificate of analysis on that liver get fruit in your diet i think that's the best source of carbohydrates for humans alongside raw honey get some raw dairy if you can from a good farm and i think you've got a great diet for a pregnant woman i think in terms of heart and soil supplements if you can't or won't include liver or heart in your diet start with something like beef organs add skin hair and nails to that and even consider adding lifeblood to that lifeblood has blood and liver and spleen a lot of women do get a anemia of pregnancy which is dilutional anemia but getting enough heme iron in your diet during pregnancy lifeblood is the best source of that i actually love life but as a pre-workout as well for me but i think life blood beef organs skin hair nails will be a great place to start for a pregnancy stack from heart and soil for that pregnancy and steve goes on to ask the second question how and when would you transition infants from breast milk to an animal based diet what food items would you introduce first how much fruit or honey would be advisable when would you introduce organ meat and or heart and soil supplements so i'm going to leave the transition of breast milk to an animal-based diet up to every parent listening to this but i think that at six months you can begin supplementing breast milk with food and i think the first foods for infants should be cooked organs cooked meat desiccated organs from hardened soil and a little bit of fruit at six months you cannot give a child raw honey you must wait at least one year because of possible clostridial spores and that honey and an infant's digestive system is not equipped to deal with those clostridial spores in a raw honey at six months to a year of age but any age beyond that should be fine adults have no problems with this in raw honey but you cannot give raw honey to an infant so at six months i think it's fine to start introducing food along with the breast milk i would recommend breastfeeding for as long as you can that's going to be a decision for mom mom's nutrition is critically important mom needs to make sure she's getting enough calories and enough nutrients during this period as well what food items would you introduce first i answered that one meat cooked organs heart and soil supplements would be great maybe some fruit that's a great first thing to introduce for babies how much fruit or honey would be advisable yes that is probably likely

chunks.title: Contamination Concerns in Animal Liver Consumption
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses concerns over contaminants such as cadmium and PFAS in deer liver, cautioning listeners about sourcing organ meats and the potential risks associated with pollution from agricultural practices.

chunks.title: Fruit Consumption Quantities and Recommendations
chunks.summary: In response to concerns about fruit intake, the speaker suggests that active individuals can consume more fruit without negative effects, advising that fruit should complement a balanced animal-based diet. They recommend adjusting fruit intake based on individual activity levels.

then also if they're being fed to cattle and livestock as a part of poor quality feed that's going to increase the pfas's in the food chain now let's talk about chicken and pork you guys may know that i've had conversations with my friend anthony gustin about chicken feed it's very hard to find a good chicken feed hopefully we'll remedy that at some point in the future but what are your but what are the chickens you're eating fed well if they're fed regular chicken feed we know that that chicken feed is probably containing seed oils is it does it contain pieces of plastic or scraps of paper is it low quality what is the what are the pfas what are the pfas levels in that chicken feed how much of that pfas contamination is ending up in the eggs it's all quite possible so i say this not to make you afraid i say this to empower you with knowledge i believe that in the coming future we will increasingly hear about pfases i think these are one of the greatest threats to our health seed oils processed sugars biggest first threats but pfases are the new bpas and even with bpas bisphenol a there are now bpe and bps and these other bisphenol compounds that are similarly endocrine disruptive so these endocrine disruptors these environmental contaminants pfases bisphenol type compounds these are the things we must go to great lengths to avoid as much as possible if we truly want to be healthy as humans so this is a great question from nick while we are talking about raw milk i wanted to share a study that i found really interesting there are multiple studies like this in the literature um but as i was doing some research for content for instagram i came across a number of literature reviews and studies which are observational and i'll talk about that in a moment looking at raw milk so you can see in this study raw cow's milk and its protective effect on allergies and asthma and they found that they present the current understanding of the protective effect of raw milk on allergies and asthma there's a significant body of epidemiologic evidence that consumption of raw milk in childhood and adulthood in farm but also non-farm populations can be one of the most protective factors against allergy and asthma i think this probably has to do with the probiotic compounds found in raw milk certainly when raw milk is not handled properly it can be a source of food contamination but for me if i can get it from a good source the benefits outweigh the risks if you can obtain raw milk that's a decision you have to make for yourself but there is really clear good evidence that raw milk is beneficial and protective for hay fever eczema asthma and other allergic conditions probably because being exposed to those bacteria in the milk as a child and adult is beneficial for our immune system for our gut microbiome the delicate interplay of these things throughout our lives so raw milk is great understand the protective sourcing some people when i talk about this epidemiology with regard to raw milk will point out that i am not a fan of epidemiology with regard to vegetables and say look at this guy he's a hypocrite so i wanted to clarify that position i wanted to clarify that position in this moment as well when i look at epidemiology regarding vegetables there are a couple of factors to take into play that i think make that epidemiology highly suspect and highly at risk of being confounded by what we call healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias specifically if you look at the epidemiology regarding vegetables in human health or meat and human health there are disparate results across u.s western and eastern populations this makes me highly suspect there's something else going on i don't believe meat is good for asians and bad for the u.s and i don't believe that vegetables are necessarily good for humans based on epidemiology studies that could be confounded by what we call a healthy user bias there is a real compelling narrative that must be disproved with interventional studies here when we look at epidemiology we must be aware of the possibility of healthy and unhealthy user bias we must look at the people eating vegetables and ask is there a consistent narrative over the last 70 years i would argue there is that says vegetables are healthy so the people who eat more vegetables are also likely to be people who are doing more healthy behaviors could that be having an effect could that be making the vegetables look like they are beneficial to those people when in fact it is their healthy behaviors yes and if you've listened to any of my content you know there are many analyses of this epidemiology that shows exactly that specifically in the uk shopper study if you look at both vegetarians and omnivores we find that when the healthy behaviors are accounted for they both live the same amount of time meaning that there was no protective effect of eating more vegetables or excluding meat from the diet when they looked at which groups were doing healthy behaviors the omnivores who had healthy behaviors lived a long amount of time and the vegetarians who had healthy behaviors lived longer than the average person in the uk as well so the protective effect appears to be from the protective effect appears to be from healthy behaviors not necessarily from the absence of eating meat or from the inclusion of more vegetables in the human diet there are also many other multiple there are also many other studies i've talked about that are interventional studies looking at the inclusion of larger or significant amounts of fruit and vegetables in the diet versus lower amounts of fruit and vegetables or lower amount or zero fruit and vegetables in the human diet and there are many interventional studies which are much more revealing than these epidemiology studies which suggest that those vegetables are not necessarily protective for humans and the results of those studies many times suggest that that inclusion of fruit and vegetables didn't necessarily have a protective effect for those humans i've shown those interventional studies many times in the past so all of this data together makes me highly suspicious of the epidemiology regarding vegetables in human health and i think it is highly likely it is confounded by healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias when i look at epidemiology like this regarding raw milk and its protective effect on allergies and asthma the first thing i think is well maybe it's being on the farm maybe more people on the farm are drinking raw milk and this is a type of healthy user bias but in the abstract they say they have control for this and that people who are not on farms also see a benefit associated with raw milk so i can't come up with a more compelling hypothesis for why raw milk is protective for humans than the fact that it is actually protected for humans because of the probiotics in the milk the difference here is that when we are looking at epidemiology we must be careful to account for the possibility of healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias and when i look at epidemiology if i can't draw a compelling story or i can't figure out where the healthy user bias or unhealthy user bias could be confounding the study it makes me much more suspicious that it makes me much more confident that the association may be true the association still needs to be tested with interventional studies we can never draw causative inference from this observational research but it gives me more faith in the association in that situation and i may talk about it so this is the difference between vegetable studies that are epidemiological and things like raw milk if you guys have been following my content recently you know that i've been talking about an epidemiology study that linked that associated fish consumption with increased rates of melanoma let's use this one as another example here can i come up with a compelling narrative by which people who eat more fish may have an increased risk of melanoma i really can't perhaps you can you can put it in the comments there's probably a healthy user bias there the people that eat more fish probably believe that fish is healthy for them and yet they're having an unhealthy complication specifically increased rates of melanoma so where might the healthy user bias or the unhealthy user bias be hiding in this epidemiology i can't think of it which makes me find this association quite compelling and then we can say and there are many plausible explanations for the reason that fish might increase the rates of melanoma microplastics pfases and things like heavy metals or pcbs polychlorinated biphenyls in the fish that could be worsening cancer rates specifically melanoma so this finding was really taken with a lot of disbelief and incredulity by the nutrition community was widely ignored because everyone knows that fish is so healthy because red meat is so bad for you and i'm just laughing in the background thinking you guys actually need to realize that this fish is not good for humans and this is a good indication of this but i wish that most of this epidemiology were then followed up with interventional studies that's what we really need we need more interventional studies to test whether the inclusion of vegetables in the human diet is actually beneficial for humans or not we need more interventional studies to test whether meat is beneficial or harmful for humans i've shown in this podcast many times that they're that of the existing intervention studies we have red meat looks to be very healthy for humans no surprise there it's what we've been favoring for our entire existence as humans and we need more interventional studies to look at raw milk and see if it does protect humans for allergies and asthma the problem is that some of these studies must be done longitudinally which takes a long time we need to figure out how to address that in human health and nutritional research because so many of these associations can be valuable but we must be careful not to be fooled by healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias all right i'll do one more question to wrap up this ama for you guys this week steve asks how would you construct a diet and harden soil stack for a pregnant woman i think i had heard you mentioned bone marrow and liver is one of the recommendations would skin hair and nails or any others be advisable for prenatal as well as during pregnancy so i've done a podcast recently with lindsay milis in which we talk about a diocese in which we talk about dietary recommendations for prenatal conception pregnancy and postpartum i believe that an animal-based diet focused on organs and meat and fruit and honey is ideal across the lifespan i believe that an animal-based diet of organs and meat and fruit and honey is ideal across the human lifespan and that whether you're feeding a child or you're feeding a woman who's looking to conceive or a woman who is pregnant these are an ideal set of foods for those people now there's going to be some variation in terms of calories needed someone who is pregnant is going to be very careful not to eat too few calories and so they might want to increase the carbohydrates they might want to increase the fats but i think that as i've said in the past for a pregnant woman you want to think about one gram of protein per pound of body weight that is a combination of organs and meat and that is for goal body weight pregnant women should not be looking to lose weight so that when they get pregnant they're probably at their goal body weight one gram of protein per pound of body weight remember that one pound of meat is 100 grams of protein start there get organs a little bit of liver every day half an ounce a day totally great during pregnancy contains so many critical nutrients that are essential for the pregnancy folate riboflavin all of these things choline for the developing brain and the fetus a half an ounce of liver is not going to put anybody at risk of vitamin a toxicity get that liver from a good source as i mentioned in this podcast don't just get regular calves liver from walmart get a grass-fed grass finish regenerative farm liver even ask them for a coa a certificate of analysis on that liver get fruit in your diet i think that's the best source of carbohydrates for humans alongside raw honey get some raw dairy if you can from a good farm and i think you've got a great diet for a pregnant woman i think in terms of heart and soil supplements if you can't or won't include liver or heart in your diet start with something like beef organs add skin hair and nails to that and even consider adding lifeblood to that lifeblood has blood and liver and spleen a lot of women do get a anemia of pregnancy which is dilutional anemia but getting enough heme iron in your diet during pregnancy lifeblood is the best source of that i actually love life but as a pre-workout as well for me but i think life blood beef organs skin hair nails will be a great place to start for a pregnancy stack from heart and soil for that pregnancy and steve goes on to ask the second question how and when would you transition infants from breast milk to an animal based diet what food items would you introduce first how much fruit or honey would be advisable when would you introduce organ meat and or heart and soil supplements so i'm going to leave the transition of breast milk to an animal-based diet up to every parent listening to this but i think that at six months you can begin supplementing breast milk with food and i think the first foods for infants should be cooked organs cooked meat desiccated organs from hardened soil and a little bit of fruit at six months you cannot give a child raw honey you must wait at least one year because of possible clostridial spores and that honey and an infant's digestive system is not equipped to deal with those clostridial spores in a raw honey at six months to a year of age but any age beyond that should be fine adults have no problems with this in raw honey but you cannot give raw honey to an infant so at six months i think it's fine to start introducing food along with the breast milk i would recommend breastfeeding for as long as you can that's going to be a decision for mom mom's nutrition is critically important mom needs to make sure she's getting enough calories and enough nutrients during this period as well what food items would you introduce first i answered that one meat cooked organs heart and soil supplements would be great maybe some fruit that's a great first thing to introduce for babies how much fruit or honey would be advisable yes that is probably likely age and weight dependent i think for kids see how much meat they'll eat i would offer the fruit second because they often will just want to eat the sweet fruit and then they will not eat enough meat if you give them the meat first they'll fill up on meat give them as much meat as they want maybe add a little butter to it a little salt they'll eat tons of it i've seen this with my niece and nephew then offer some fruit along with that and i would introduce oregon meats as cooked organ meats either mixed into their burgers or something or desiccated organs from hardened soil sprinkled on their food as early as you can six months is fine in my opinion so that leads me to one other question that i will address before i wrap up this podcast a lot of people ask how much fruit is too much for humans i think you will know how much fruit you should be eating with some intuition as i said the new carnivore md website will be up very soon there is a calculator there that will give you some broad inclination of macros that is we're going to help you break it down protein fat and carbohydrates based on your activity level and your desired body composition but for me for instance i'm eating 200 plus grams of carbohydrates a day i eat a whole pineapple i eat at least a mango i'm eating at least a whole papaya a day i'm having multiple tablespoons of honey and this is in addition to carbohydrates that i'm certainly getting from raw milk and raw cream when you add it all up i think i'm safely above 200 grams of carbohydrates every single day but i'm also a very active human the amount of fruit that is too much for you is when you're not hungry enough to eat the other things that are nutrient dense in your diet i think most people can eat as much fruit as they want provided that they are getting the meat and the organs and the other critical sources of nutrients in their diet i don't think you need to fear the fruit if you're very active eat more fruit if you're very active include more honey in your diet as well that's fine but scale it based on your activity i think your body will tell you uh intuitively i suspect this will work out for many of you listening that if you are more active you may crave more carbohydrates and i think that's fine if you are less active i bet if you listen to your cravings you will find that you need less carbohydrates in your diet and

chunks.title: Closing Thoughts and Community Engagement
chunks.summary: The speaker wraps up the podcast, encouraging listeners to join the Animal Based 30 challenge and emphasizing the importance of discussions on nutrition and community health. They express enthusiasm for the benefits of an animal-based diet.
